transcript.clean_text: Welcome back to GCN Racing and highlights of stage 19 of La Vuelta a España. Yesterday was the penultimate day in the mountains and there was yet more success for the youngsters. 22-year-old Sergio Higuita taking his first pro win just 5 months after signing his first World Tour contract. Behind him the GC battle was as fierce as ever and whilst there was no shaking Roglic out of his red jersey, Miguel Ángel López had done enough to prize the white jersey off the shoulders of Tadej Pogačar who finished just under 1 minute behind him. That's how the GC now looks, Valverde up to second, Quintana down to third. Today it didn't look too tough on paper but once again the weather looked set to play its part. There was a small bump in the form of a third category climb at the start but that was the only categorized hurdle of the day. There was however a nasty climb to the finish line, gradients reaching up to 12% in the last 700 m, some of which was on cobbles. It didn't take long for the group to go clear after the neutral start this morning. 11 riders in total, here they are on the climb. Dillier, Novak, Cavagna, Craddock, Armirail, Greipel, O'Connor, De la Cruz, Arndt, Steśna and finally Shane Archbold. The weather had already taken a turn for the worse quite early on in the stage. And here you can see CCC already at the front of the bunch setting the pace and making sure that the gap didn't grow out too much. They were one of the teams who'd missed it and with Bora and Deceuninck having representation in the breakaway, they weren't going to get much help in bringing it back. With 66 K's to go, we had a major crash and it involved the race leader Roglic and a number of his teammates. It looked nasty and unfortunately this resulted in Tony Martin abandoning the race. Lots of looking behind the riders who'd got through it trying to establish who hadn't. It was some time in fact before Roglic would get back to his feet and back on his bike and he'd have a hard old chase in front of him. Mainly down to the fact that Movistar had chosen to accelerate at the front. Not normally the done thing. They almost single-handedly melted social media. Movistar were basically ripping up the unwritten rules and ripping up the race which was in pieces. Also caught out was Miguel Ángel López. Good news for Roglic though who was once again able to rely on Astana to do the chasing for him. The gap though over a minute between the two groups. And they still weren't holding back at the front. In fact if anything they were going even harder. Behind you can see the decimated peloton spread down the road. Roglic would eventually find more allies in other teams, all presumably equally unhappy at what Movistar were doing in front. Fast forward another couple of K's and somebody at the team had come to their senses. They would never have lived that down if they'd continued to the finish. And so Roglic eventually was able to make his way back on whilst Valverde was being given a stern telling off by Astana. He'll be on the naughty step later on tonight. And that slowing was good news too for the breakaway whose lead started to go back in the right direction. They were down to 10 riders by this point as Archbold had been called back to the bunch. 20 seconds had been shaved off by the time the peloton turned this corner with 37 K's to go as they faced yet more crosswinds and another split in the bunch. This time all the major players apart from Roglic had safely made that front group. Up front Cavagna could sense the danger of the bunch closing in on them behind and decided to go it alone. A bold move with 25 K's still remaining, most of it into a stiff headwind. But it was looking like a good decision as he hit the outskirts of Toledo he'd still maintain an advantage of over a minute. Behind Deceuninck obviously weren't going to chase but Lotto Soudal, CCC and Bora-Hansgrohe were doing their bit. Was it too little too late though? They hadn't yet caught the rest of the breakaway who were still sitting around 30 seconds behind the lone leader. Nikias Arndt and Lawson Craddock using a small climb to make one final bid for stage victory. They were later joined by Groupama-FDJ's Armirail but with the gap holding steady at 30 seconds it was going to take a monumental effort to catch Cavagna in front. Well, what a year it's been again for Deceuninck-Quick Step. Cavagna spends the majority of his time working for others as he has done at this very race. It was incredible that he was still able to find the energy to do this today. Onto the finishing climb and the cobblestones of Toledo and Cavagna had it all but sewn up. One glance back on the finishing straight and he could see that despite the speeding group behind he was about to take his first Grand Tour stage victory. And my word didn't he deserve that. A 25 K solo effort, chapeau. Behind a disappointed Sam Bennett would prove his strength on tough finishes by being best of the rest but Deceuninck-Quick Step proved their all-round strength by also finishing third and fourth with Stybar and Gilbert respectively. A third pro win then for this 24-year-old and as I said his first in a Grand Tour and for his team that marked their 61st win of the season so far. There's your top 10. It was Valverde in fifth, a 3 second gap back to sixth with Tosh Van der Sande, Tuens, Pogačar, López and Roglic rounding out the top 10. Well, it's another step closer to Madrid for this man but another stressful stage. One more big one's come tomorrow and this is how it stands going into it. He now has a 2 minute and 47 second advantage over Valverde whilst the rest of the top 10 remains unchanged. Tomorrow it's the final day in the mountains. It's a long one and it's a tough one too. 190 K's, six categorized climbs and a summit finish at the Plataforma de Gredos. This really is the last chance for any change in the GC so we can expect or at least hope for fireworks. My prediction for the stage win is Miguel Ángel López. Come on You can do it my son. See you tomorrow.
